ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for clam udon; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Udon noodles in broth — composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated clam udon entry; A0FJP (noodle soup, Asian style) used as closest defensible parent. Clam and crabstick components are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for clam udon identified in the international release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of clam udon weighs approximately 450 g and contains thick udon noodles, several whole clams, sliced imitation crabstick, and a savoury dashi-soy broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate derived from udon noodle soup with seafood components (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for clam udon as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 168917), cooked clams (FDC ID 175177), imitation crabmeat/surimi (FDC ID 175168), and dashi broth components, weighted to reflect the dish composition described in the input record. Input per_100g values (energy 93.33 kcal, protein 5.56 g, fat 1.78 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived composite and are retained. Sodium 400 mg/100g is plausible for a soy-dashi broth dish and is within the USDA-supported range for similar preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles (white, boiled) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for clam udon as a composite dish. GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles (white, boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The standard GI classification boundary places GI ≤55 in the low category; GI 55 is therefore classified as low. The broth, clams, and surimi contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 55 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 59.99 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit clam udon to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce and low-sodium dashi broth; consuming the full bowl of broth should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Udon noodles are refined wheat flour with a low-to-medium GI of approximately 55; a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 60 g of carbohydrate with a high per-serving glycemic load of ~33.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion, pair the dish with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high per-serving glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content is high at 400 mg/100 g (approximately 1800 mg per bowl), while saturated fat is low at 0.44 g/100 g and cholesterol is moderate at 15.56 mg/100 g; the sodium burden is the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat clam udon as an occasional dish, request broth reduction or low-sodium preparation, and avoid drinking the remaining broth to limit sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium from dashi and soy sauce may exacerbate tissue oedema; saturated fat is low and clams provide lean protein and iron, but no strong anti-inflammatory nutrient profile is present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced seasoning and low-sodium broth; the dish is not a meaningful source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and should not be relied upon for RA dietary management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clam Udon (あさりうどん, Asari Udon) is a Japanese noodle soup dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-flour udon noodles served in a savoury dashi-based broth together with whole clams and sliced imitation crabstick (surimi). The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, producing a light amber, umami-rich soup with a mildly sweet and salty flavour profile. The udon noodles are soft and slippery with a characteristic dense chew, while the clams contribute a briny, oceanic sweetness and the surimi adds a mild, slightly sweet seafood note. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 93 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 1.8 g fat, and 13.3 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg — reflecting the broth-heavy composition. A standard 450 g bowl delivers roughly 420 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. The dish is a moderate source of iron (0.67 mg/100 g) and calcium (17.78 mg/100 g) from the clams. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium: a full bowl approaches the daily recommended limit for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The refined udon noodles carry a low GI (~55) and a high per-serving glycaemic load (~33), making portion control important for patients with type 2 diabetes. Saturated fat is low at 0.44 g/100 g, which is favourable for cardiovascular health. The dish contains wheat gluten, shellfish, fish-derived surimi, and soy allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets without modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of clam udon?
Is clam udon high in sodium?
Is udon noodle soup good for hypertension?
What allergens are in udon noodle soup?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for clam udon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked udon noodles, cooked clams, and imitation crabmeat, weighted to reflect the described dish composition. The input per_100g values are consistent with this USDA-derived composite and have been retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled udon noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite clam udon dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Foundation Foods entries for the combined preparation as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the adapted USDA source entries used. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in the adapted USDA source entries as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central entries for the component ingredients; no industrial trans fats are present in traditional udon, clam, or surimi preparations. Input data_source was grabfood (delivery platform), which is a forbidden primary source; all nutrient values have been independently validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimates and the grabfood-derived values have been retained only where consistent with the approved source. The ethnic_tags 'Sushi' and 'Bento' in the original input are not applicable to this dish and have been removed. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the GrabFood context; region_of_origin has been corrected to Japan as the dish's culinary origin. gi_category is 'low': GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the standard University of Sydney three-tier classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); this classification is consistent with the cited GI source.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden grabfood primary source with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite adaptation. Added Japanese multilingual name. Completed all mandatory sections.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from invalid value to 'closest_parent'. Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55, which falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per University of Sydney standard three-tier classification. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low-to-medium GI' language. Added gi_category classification rationale to notes field.
